title: Supporting the designation of March 2025 as Endometriosis Awareness Month.
plain_summary: This bill supports making March 2025 a special month to raise awareness about endometriosis. Endometriosis is a painful disease that affects one out of every ten women who can have babies. It often takes doctors a long time to figure out that women have this disease, and it can make their lives and jobs much harder. The House of Representatives wants to help by supporting more research, education, and better treatments since there is no cure for this condition yet.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution supports making March 2025 Endometriosis Awareness Month.

sections.title: Background Information
sections.text: Endometriosis is a disease where tissue that normally grows inside the uterus also grows outside of it. This disease affects one in ten women who can have children, making it one of the most common women's health problems. No one knows what causes endometriosis, but it is more likely to happen if your mother, sister, or daughter has it. It is also more common in women who started their periods early, have short monthly cycles, or have heavy periods that last more than seven days. The only way doctors can be completely sure someone has endometriosis is through a surgery called laparoscopy. Many women wait three to eleven years from when they first feel pain until they get the right diagnosis. This long wait makes their lives worse and costs a lot of money. For many girls and women, endometriosis is a lifelong disease that hurts their relationships, school, work, ability to have children, and daily life. The main symptoms are pain and trouble getting pregnant. Many women with endometriosis live with severe, ongoing pain. About seventy five out of one hundred women with endometriosis get the wrong diagnosis at first. Doctors can treat the symptoms with surgery, hormone treatments, and other medicines. Endometriosis makes healthcare more expensive and creates a big burden for patients. In the United States, each patient with endometriosis costs more than thirteen thousand dollars per year in medical care. Forty out of one hundred women with endometriosis say it hurts their career growth. About fifty out of one hundred women with endometriosis have trouble doing their jobs. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention found that women with endometriosis spend an average of eighteen days per year in bed because of their condition. Women with endometriosis lose eleven hours of work each week because they cannot be as productive. The physical and mental effects of endometriosis hurt all parts of life, including social life, relationships, and work. Medical groups and patient organizations say there needs to be more public attention and better educational materials about this unmet health need for women. There is a need for more research and updated treatment guidelines for endometriosis. There is an ongoing need for more clinical studies and treatment options to help with this painful disease. There is no known cure for endometriosis.

sections.title: What the House of Representatives Decides
sections.text: The House of Representatives strongly supports the goals and ideas of Endometriosis Awareness Month. The House recognizes the need to find and treat endometriosis early, teach healthcare providers more about it, and provide better care that respects different cultures. The House remains committed to supporting and funding research on endometriosis to find better treatments, help women get pregnant, and ultimately find a cure. The House encourages all people in the United States to observe the month with proper awareness and educational activities.
